perturbation.original_text: The Parties shall limit disclosure of Confidential Information within its own organization to its directors, officers, partners, members and/or employees having a need to know Confidential Information and shall not disclose Confidential Information to any third party (whether an individual, corporation, or other entity) without the prior written consent of the other party, which consent shall not be unreasonably withheld.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties shall endeavor to limit disclosure of Confidential Information within its own organization to its directors, officers, partners, members and/or employees having a need to know Confidential Information and should consider not disclosing Confidential Information to any third party (whether an individual, corporation, or other entity) without the prior written consent of the other party.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'shall limit' to 'shall endeavor to limit' and 'shall not disclose' to 'should consider not disclosing' weakens the obligation, making it ambiguous whether the parties are strictly bound to prevent disclosure to third parties. This undermines the intent of the NDA to protect confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects trade secrets through injunctive relief and damages for misappropriation. By weakening the obligation to limit disclosure, the modified text increases the risk of misappropriation. The original text clearly prohibits disclosure to third parties absent consent, which is consistent with protecting trade secrets. The modified text is vague and does not provide sufficient protection, conflicting with the requirements to actively prevent trade secret misappropriation to avail oneself of the law's protections.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially conflicting with trade secret law. The original text uses 'shall limit' and 'shall not disclose,' creating a clear prohibition. The changed text uses 'shall endeavor to limit' and 'should consider not disclosing,' making the obligation ambiguous. Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' as disclosure or use of a trade secret without consent. By weakening the restriction on disclosure, the modified text increases the risk of misappropriation, potentially undermining CUTSA protection.

perturbation.original_text: All notices hereunder shall be in writing and shall be deemed to have been duly given if delivered personally, one day after delivery to a nationally recognized overnight delivery service, charges prepaid, or three days after being sent by registered or certified mail, postage prepaid, to the parties at their respective addresses set forth above and:
perturbation.changed_text: All notices hereunder should ideally be in writing and may be considered given if delivered in some manner, potentially including personal delivery, overnight service, or standard mail, to the parties at their addresses.
perturbation.explanation: This revision replaces concrete requirements ('shall', 'one day', 'three days', 'registered or certified mail') with vague suggestions ('should ideally', 'may be considered', 'some manner', 'potentially including'). This creates ambiguity about what constitutes adequate notice, hindering legal enforcement.
perturbation.law_explanation: CCP § 1013 specifies how to calculate service by mail, including adding additional time for responses based on the location of service. The modified text's vagueness about acceptable methods of delivery and timing directly contradicts the specificity of CCP § 1013, potentially invalidating legal notices served under the contract and complicating legal proceedings.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1013. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 5. Notices, and Filing and Service of Papers [1010 - 1020] ( Chapter 5 enacted 1872. ) 1013. (a) In case of service by mail, the notice or other paper shall be deposited in a post office, mailbox, subpost office, substation, or mail chute, or other like facility regularly maintained by the United States Postal Service, in a sealed envelope, with postage paid, addressed to the person on whom it is to be served, at the office address as last given by that person on any document filed in the cause and served on the party making service by mail; otherwise at that party’s place of residence. Service is complete at the time of the deposit, but any period of notice and any right or duty to do any act or make any response within any period or on a date certain after service of the document, which time period or date is prescribed by statute or rule of court, shall be extended five calendar days, upon service by mail, if the place of address and the place of mailing is within the State of California, 10 calendar days if either the place of mailing or the place of address is outside the State of Californi
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces specific notice requirements with vague suggestions, contradicting CCP § 1013. The original text mandates specific delivery methods and timelines (e.g., 'one day after delivery to a nationally recognized overnight delivery service'). The changed text uses terms like 'should ideally be in writing' and 'may be considered given if delivered in some manner,' which undermine the original contract's and the law's intention for clear and enforceable notice. CCP 1013(a) specifies how service by mail should be executed ('deposited in a post office...in a sealed envelope, with postage paid'), including details for calculating when service is complete and extending response times. The modified text's ambiguity regarding acceptable delivery methods and timing directly clashes with the specificity of CCP § 1013, potentially invalidating legal notices served under the contract.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of any dispute involving this Agreement, the parties hereby agree that such dispute shall be resolved in the appropriate State or Federal court, in the county of Los Angeles, state of California, and the parties hereby agree that proper venue and jurisdiction shall lie with any court of competent jurisdiction in the county of Los Angeles, state of California.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of a dispute possibly involving this Agreement, the parties may consider resolving such dispute in a potentially relevant court located somewhere near Los Angeles, California; the parties hope that venue and jurisdiction might be proper in some court located in the vicinity of Los Angeles, California.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly designates Los Angeles courts for dispute resolution. The modified text introduces uncertainty with terms like 'possibly', 'may consider', 'potentially relevant', 'somewhere near', and 'might be proper', making the venue clause unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: CCP § 395 establishes rules for venue in California courts. The modified text's deliberate vagueness undermines the parties' agreement on venue. The law requires a definite place of trial. By suggesting potentially relevant courts located 'somewhere near' Los Angeles, and hoping venue 'might be proper', the modified clause introduces confusion and contradicts the requirement for a defined venue.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 395. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. OF THE PLACE OF TRIAL, RECLASSIFICATION, AND COORDINATION OF CIVIL ACTIONS [392 - 404.9] ( Heading of Title 4 amended by Stats. 1999, Ch. 344, Sec. 6. ) CHAPTER 1. Place of Trial [392 - 403] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1999, Ch. 344, Sec. 7. ) 395. (a) Except as otherwise provided by law and subject to the power of the court to transfer actions or proceedings as provided in this title, the superior court in the county where the defendants or some of them reside at the commencement of the action is the proper court for the trial of the action. If the action is for injury to person or personal property or for death from wrongful act or negligence, the superior court in either the county where the injury occurs or the injury causing death occurs or the county where the defendants, or some of them reside at the commencement of the action, is a proper court for the trial of the action. In a proceeding for dissolution of marriage, the superior court in the county where either the petitioner or respondent has been a resident for three months next preceding the commencement of the proceeding is the proper court for the trial of the proceeding. In a proceeding for nulli
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies the venue for dispute resolution as courts in Los Angeles, California. The changed text uses ambiguous language ('possibly', 'may consider', 'potentially relevant', 'somewhere near', 'might be proper') that contradicts the requirement for a definite venue. CCP § 395, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, establishes rules for venue in California courts. The altered text's vagueness undermines the agreement on venue, contradicting the need for a defined place of trial.

perturbation.original_text: The validity, construction and performance of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws, without regard to the laws as choice or conflict of laws, of the state of California.
perturbation.changed_text: The interpretation of this Agreement could possibly be subject to certain laws vaguely related to California.
perturbation.explanation: The original text establishes California law as the governing law. The revised language replaces the definite 'shall be governed' with the ambiguous 'could possibly be subject' and 'vaguely related to', creating uncertainty about which jurisdiction's laws apply.
perturbation.law_explanation: Cal. Civ. Code § 1646 states that a contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed, or, if it indicates a place of performance, according to the law and usage of the place where it is made. The modified text creates ambiguity in governing law, rendering this statute difficult to apply and leading to uncertainty in how the contract will be interpreted and enforced. There would be no defined rule for interpreting the contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1646. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1646. A contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed; or, if it does not indicate a place of performance, according to the law and usage of the place where it is made. (Enacted 1872.)

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces significant ambiguity regarding the governing law, directly contradicting the intent of Cal. Civ. Code § 1646. The original text clearly states that California law 'shall be governed,' while the changed text uses vague terms like 'could possibly be subject' and 'vaguely related to.' This undermines the statute's purpose, which is to provide a definite rule for interpreting the contract, as stated in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states, 'A contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed; or, if it does not indicate a place of performance, according to the law and usage of the place where it is made.' The changed text's ambiguity makes it unclear which place's law should govern, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of a breach, or threatened breach of any provision of this Agreement, the non-breaching party shall give the breaching party a notice to cure within three (3) business days. In the event the breach is not cured, the non-breaching party shall have the right to bring appropriate legal action in the appropriate State or Federal court as set forth in Paragraph 9 above.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of a possible breach of some provision of this Agreement, the non-breaching party might perhaps give the breaching party some form of notice, possibly to cure within an undefined amount of time. If some breach is not addressed, the non-breaching party could maybe bring some sort of action in a court.
perturbation.explanation: The original text sets out a clear process for addressing breaches. The revised language replaces the definite 'shall give' with the uncertain 'might perhaps give', 'three (3) business days' with 'undefined amount of time', 'shall have the right' with 'could maybe', and 'appropriate legal action' with 'some sort of action', making the breach remedy unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Cal. Civ. Code § 3300 outlines the measure of damages for breach of contract. By rendering the breach notification and remedy provisions vague and uncertain, the modified text makes it difficult or impossible for a non-breaching party to effectively pursue damages under § 3300. The lack of a defined cure period and the uncertainty about the non-breaching party's right to take action impede the determination of damages resulting from a breach.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3300. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. COMPENSATORY RELIEF [3281 - 3361] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 2. Measure of Damages [[3300.] - 3361] ( Chapter 2 enacted 1872. ) ARTICLE 1. Damages for Breach of Contract [[3300.] - 3322] ( Article 1 enacted 1872. ) [3300.] Section Thirty-three Hundred. For the breach of an obligation arising from contract, the measure of damages, except where otherwise expressly provided by this Code, is the amount which will compensate the party aggrieved for all the detriment proximately caused thereby, or which, in the ordinary course of things, would be likely to result therefrom. (Amended by Code Amendments 1873-74, Ch. 612.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts Cal. Civ. Code § 3300 by introducing ambiguity into the breach notification and remedy provisions, which undermines the ability of the non-breaching party to pursue damages. The original text's 'shall give' and 'three (3) business days' are replaced with uncertain language, making the remedy unenforceable. Snippet 1 states that the measure of damages for breach of contract is the amount that compensates the aggrieved party for the detriment caused. The modified text's vague language hinders the determination of damages, thus creating a contradiction.
